Reminder, the Treasury market now
features disequilibrium between price
sensitive buyers and price insensitive
buyers. So, if you look at the share of
the broad Treasury debt market that is
owned by the private non-bank sector, so
people who need ex ante units of return
to take risk in their portfolios, is now
58% up 22 uh percentage points from
where it was in 2021. And this is a
function of the Fed not buying them in
fast enough, commercial banks aren't
buying them fast enough, and and foreign
central banks are actively selling them
at 15, 14 percent, respectively.
Reminder, the Treasury bond market is
not adequately pricing the high
probability risk of a substantial
increase in supply as I've been talking
about for the past 15 minutes. We're 65
basis points of term premium on the
10-year Treasury yield. The long-run
mean of term premium prior to the GFC is
almost 200 basis points.
If you want to subtract the deviation
from the current value from the long-run
mean and add it back to the Treasury
bond yield, we're talking about a five
and five and a half percent 10-year
Treasury yield. You can't buy a bond
until you persistently see five,
perhaps only to eight to 6% on the
10-year Treasury.
You can't.
You can't. We arrived at this conclusion
in 2023 and had the courage to pivot
KISS from 30% bonds to 30% gold uh in
target allocation terms uh back in
October of 2024.
And we will not be buying bonds until we
get a reasonable price to take on all
this risk. And we think this is one of
the biggest, most mispriced This is the
biggest mispricing in all global
financial markets. It doesn't have to
get un-mispriced in a short-term time

uh not trying to scare anybody, but this
is just the math.

In terms of our liquidity outlook,
our macro weather model currently views
the liquidity cycle as a meaningful
near-term tailwind for asset markets.
Our research views the Fed's decision to
disregard the affordability crisis and
counter deterioration in the repo market
with reserve management purchases as an
indication the Fed may be permanently
shifting to expansionary balance sheet
policy to counter the geopolitically
driven supply-demand imbalance in the
Treasury bond market. The Fed is the
only institution in the world with a
balance sheet large and flexible enough
to fill this growing void. So, that's
paradigm D, the print phase of the
cut-quote-print sequence required to
address this supply-demand imbalance.
And trust me, they will be printing at
some point. Uh
years of balance sheet run-off have left
liquidity and funding markets at or
below the Fed's desired uh ample level,
which we can determine by the, you know,
persistently positive and/or, you know,
you know, spread between SOFR and IORB,
which is very different than the
persistently negative spread we had
prior to September of last year. So,
we're just in a new regime in terms of
the amount of uh liquidity sloshing
around global financial markets. And
this lower level of liquidity is causing
issues and consternation. We Hedge funds
can't just keep accumulating leverage
and buying whatever they their best
ideas. Retail investors can't keep
accumulating margin debt and buying all
their best ideas. That accumulation of
leverage is is stopped. And so now we
have to sell things to buy other things.
And that's exactly what we've had these
big violent factor rotations, which is
exactly what we've been calling for
since early November when we first
noticed the positioning cycle dynamics
got full in the context of this. Uh
years of dovish net financing policy
have systematically reflated the TGA
balance, which has drained bank reserves
as well. So, if we look at the years of
dovish net financing policy, which was
first uh uh

in our opinion, that's two signals from
the people who sell the bonds to the
market telling you that there is a
geopolitically driven supply-demand
imbalance in the Treasury bond market
and that's why we've had such a high uh

so high cuz you have to keep rolling
